one_line_takeaway: A training-free, plug-and-play guardrail that conditions agent safety on a mined user profile and enforces it at plan/tool/response/memory checkpoints, roughly doubling F1 (0.744) and 2.5x-ing recall (0.616) over LlamaGuard3/AGrail on a purpose-built personalized-safety benchmark, but evaluated largely with GPT-4o generating, labeling, and judging its own test set.
key_finding: On the personalized-safety benchmark, PSG-Agent (GPT-4o) reaches 79.7% accuracy and 0.744 F1, versus specialized guardrails LlamaGuard3 (58.3% acc, 0.262 F1) and AGrail (53.3% acc, 0.270 F1) and direct GPT-4o (Query-Only 61.9% acc / 0.387 F1; Query+History 61.7% / 0.384). The headline gain is recall: 0.616 vs 0.153-0.248 for baselines (a 148-302% increase), while keeping the highest precision (0.939). The authors read this as evidence that baselines catch only universal/obvious risks and miss user-specific hazards, and that adding raw chat history to a plain LLM barely helps (61.7% vs 61.9%) so explicit structured profiling is what matters.
core_contribution: PSG-Agent is a training-free, plug-and-play guardrail system for LLM-based agents that makes safety enforcement (a) personalized to each user and (b) dynamic across the agent execution chain rather than a single-turn content check. Its two claimed novelties are: (1) a two-stage architecture where Stage 1 mines a typed user profile from chat history (Profile Miner) and compiles Personalized Safety Criteria / PSC with a user-specific risk score and one of four decisions (Allow, Allow-with-Guards, Refuse-with-Alternative, Refuse) via an Input Guard, and Stage 2 enforces the PSC at multiple runtime checkpoints (Plan Monitor, Tool Firewall, Response Guard, Memory Guardian); and (2) a companion benchmark (~2,900 instances across 8 scenario types) purpose-built so that the correct ALLOW/REFUSE decision flips with small changes to the user profile. The authors also frame the problem formally as a contract-constrained sequential decision problem minimizing cumulative user-specific action+response risk subject to a safety-criteria constraint G. NOTE: LlamaGuard3, AGrail, GuardAgent, Conseca, LlamaFirewall are cited prior guardrails (Related Work / baselines), NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The paper's own stated threat model is three-dimensional: 'user profile x contextual state x agent behavior'. The insight is that harm is not a property of the response text alone but of the interaction between (i) stable+dynamic user attributes (age, health, profession, OCEAN personality, emotional/psychological state), (ii) the current situational context, and (iii) the agent's full behavioral chain (plan, tool calls, memory writes, response). Two concrete risk patterns motivate the work: (a) the same action (e.g. recommending OTC painkillers, or executing a $2,000 stock trade) is safe for one user profile but harmful for another (anticoagulant user, impulsive investor); (b) cross-turn risk accumulation, where each individual step looks safe but the agent gradually drifts toward a risky decision over multiple rounds as it absorbs user feedback and invokes tools. This is a safety/misuse-and-harm threat model (protecting users from unsafe agent behavior), NOT primarily an adversarial-attacker model, though it cites systemic attacks (prompt injection, environment hijacking) as related.
limitations: (1) No dedicated limitations section in the paper. (2) Potential evaluation circularity: GPT-4o is simultaneously the benchmark generator/augmenter, the decision-consistency filter, the primary system backbone, and one of the three LLM judges; ground-truth ALLOW/REFUSE labels are themselves LLM-produced (only 200/2,900 human-checked, and inter-rater agreement reported on 100 points). (3) The 'cross-turn risk accumulation' motivation is stronger than what is directly measured: the core benchmark evaluates a per-instance ALLOW/REFUSE decision conditioned on a synthesized history, not longitudinal multi-round drift with quantified accumulation. (4) The Profile Miner, on which everything downstream depends, is weakest exactly where personalization matters most: 61.46% accuracy on Personality/Emotional-State extraction and 68.46% on demographics (vs 89.33% for explicitly-stated health/psych). (5) Some counts are inconsistent across the paper (Figure 2 vs Appendix A.2 scenario distribution; text says ~2,900 while Appendix A.2 sums to ~2,900). (6) GuardAgent and Conseca were excluded from comparison as needing domain-specific rules, so the baseline set is partial. (7) Case-study evidence is only 2 hand-picked instances; code is in an anonymous repo.

datasets_used: Authors' own personalized-safety benchmark: ~2,900 instances across 8 scenario types (Financial 412, Social 373, Medicine 322, Daily Life 348, Career 371, Education 344, Academic 373, Emotion 357 per Appendix A.2), built from 132 hand-written seeds (Financial 16, Social 16, Medicine 25, Daily Life 18, Career 12, Education 17, Academic 14, Emotion 14) via GPT-4o augmentation + filtering + dedup
Human-evaluation subset of 200 sampled instances (4 expert annotators, 50 each)
Inter-rater reliability subset of 100 instances (LLM judges vs 4 human experts)
quantitative_results: PSG-Agent (GPT-4o) main results: Accuracy 0.797, Precision 0.939, Recall 0.616, F1 0.744
Direct GPT-4o Query-Only: Accuracy 0.619, Precision 0.881, Recall 0.248, F1 0.387
Direct GPT-4o Query+Chat History: Accuracy 0.617, Precision 0.874, Recall 0.246, F1 0.384
Recall improvement of PSG-Agent stated as 148%-302% over baselines
Backbone generalization: Overall Safe Score (OSS) ranges 0.807-0.912 across GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Grok-3, Llama-3.3-70B, DeepSeek-V3; DeepSeek-V3 OSS 0.869, Llama-3.3-70B OSS 0.843, PBS 0.848-0.876

Ablation (OSS/PCS/PBS/H/RC): Full PSG-Agent 0.912/0.984/0.917/0.819/0.892; minus Input Guard 0.833/0.896/0.854/0.681/0.740; minus Plan Monitor 0.780/0.849/0.790/0.597/0.246 (refusal-clarity collapse); minus Response Guard 0.793/0.956/0.809/0.594/0.319
Single-component removal causes 8-14% drops; multiple removals 20-65% degradation
Profile Miner extraction accuracy: Demographic 68.46%, Health & Psych 89.33%, Profession/Economic/Education/Locale 72.89%, Personality & Emotional 61.46%
Human eval: 86% high decision quality (172/200 High, 28 Medium, 0 Low), 79% high personalization sensitivity (158 High, 40 Medium, 2 Low)
LLM-judge vs human Cohen's Kappa: PCS 0.90, PBS 0.88, Helpfulness 0.85, Refusal Clarity 0.88 (n=100)
baselines_compared: Llama-Guard 3 (Meta AI, 2024) - category-based harmful content filter
AGrail (Luo et al., 2025) - adaptive iteratively-optimized safety policies
Excluded by the authors: GuardAgent (Xiang et al., 2025) and Conseca (Tsai & Bagdasarian, 2025) as requiring domain-specific rules incompatible with open-domain scenarios
